Were You Asked This?
Design an e-commerce offer subscription system

Design a system that allows users to subscribe to upcoming offers on an e-commerce platform and receive real-time notifications (email, SMS, push, or in-app) when offers go live. The system should handle 1M to 5M users.

Asked at:

Twilio

Google

An e-commerce offer subscription system is a service that lets shoppers follow upcoming deals (by product, category, seller, or keyword) and get real-time notifications via email, SMS, push, or in-app when those offers go live—similar to deal alerts on Amazon, Best Buy, or price-watch tools. Users subscribe, set preferences, and expect timely, non-duplicated alerts that respect their channel choices and quiet hours.

Interviewers use this problem to probe how you design event-driven fan-out at scale, orchestrate multi-step notification workflows, and deal with bursty traffic, rate limits, idempotency, and deliverability. It tests your ability to translate product flows into durable pipelines, model subscriptions for efficient matching, and make trade-offs that meet latency and reliability SLOs for 1–5M users without over-engineering.

Community Solutions
Design an e-commerce offer subscription system
by Jujabi
1
3
Design an e-commerce offer subscription system
by SmallHarlequinMarsupial210
0
1
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to subscribe to upcoming offers by product, category, seller, or keyword and choose preferred notification channels (email, SMS, push, in-app).

Users should be able to manage preferences such as quiet hours, per-channel opt-in/out, and unsubscribe at any time.

Users should receive real-time notifications when subscribed offers go live, with guarantees to avoid duplicate alerts for the same offer.

Users should be able to view an in-app inbox or history of offers they were notified about.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you fan out notifications to millions of subscribers quickly when an offer goes live while staying within third-party provider rate limits?

How do you schedule go-live triggers and guarantee exactly-once (or effective-once) delivery per user per offer across retries and failures?

How will you model subscriptions and efficiently match users to offers (including keyword subscriptions) at go-live time?

How do you ensure deliverability, observability, and user experience quality across channels (bounces, failures, quiet hours, frequency caps)?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Offers go live at specific moments and require low-latency fan-out across channels. Real-time update patterns help trigger and propagate events reliably to millions of recipients without tight coupling.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Each notification is a workflow: expand subscribers, filter by preferences, enqueue per-channel sends, handle retries/bounces, and record outcomes. Treating it as a multi-step saga improves reliability and observability.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

A single popular offer can cause a write storm and provider contention. Applying backpressure, rate limiting, batching, and partitioning prevents hotspots and protects downstream systems.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides durable, high-throughput event logs to buffer bursty fan-out, decouple offer go-live events from senders, support retries, and scale consumer groups by channel or partition.

Read More
Redis

Redis is ideal for low-latency deduplication keys (offerId-userId-channel), rate limiting (token buckets per provider), distributed locks for leadership, and caching hot subscriber sets.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL reliably stores users, subscriptions, preferences, and a send ledger with transactional integrity and auditing; at 1–5M users it can handle core OLTP needs with proper indexing and partitioning.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Price Tracking Service

Both systems let users subscribe to product-related events (price drops or offers) and send alerts across channels, requiring efficient subscription matching and deduplicated, timely notifications.

Read
Practice
Metrics Monitoring

Alerting platforms fan out notifications based on rules, enforce dedupe and rate limits, and require strong reliability and observability, mirroring the notification pipeline and SLO needs here.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Provider and user-level rate limiting is critical to control bursty sends; building a global throttle that works across instances directly parallels the challenges in this system.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Synchronous blast from the offer service to email/SMS/push providers

Coupling the go-live path to downstream sends creates cascading failures under load, violates provider rate limits, and eliminates backpressure. It will fail under a popular offer’s burst.

No idempotency or deduplication for per-user-per-offer sends

Retries or concurrent workers will generate duplicate notifications, degrading user trust and increasing cost. You need dedupe keys and a persistent send ledger to achieve effective-once delivery.

Ignoring provider limits, retries, and DLQs

Assuming providers always accept messages leads to data loss and poor deliverability. Without throttling, exponential backoff, and dead-letter handling, transient failures turn into outages and lost notifications.
